blocks.text: Decisive, by Chip Heath & Dan Heath. The book map.

The quality of your decisions depends far less on analysis than on process — and most people have no process at all. When Dan Lovallo and Olivier Sibony studied 1,048 business decisions, how teams decided mattered about six times more than how deeply they analyzed. Gut instinct fails on unfamiliar choices; pros-and-cons lists fail because they feed biased inputs into a neat format.

The Heaths organize the book around four villains of decision making: narrow framing (you consider too few options), confirmation bias (you gather self-serving evidence), short-term emotion (you decide while agitated or attached), and overconfidence (you assume you know how the future will unfold).

Against them they build the WRAP process: Widen your options, Reality-test your assumptions, Attain distance before deciding, and Prepare to be wrong. Each step is a set of concrete techniques backed by named research — multitracking, disconfirming questions, base rates, ooching, premortems, tripwires.

Chapter 1 introduces the villains and the framework. Chapters 2–3 cover widening options, 4–5 cover reality-testing (plus small experiments), 6 covers emotional distance and core priorities, and 7–8 cover preparing for error, setting tripwires, and why a trusted process — not certainty — is what makes people decisive.
How to choose

Chapter 1 is essential for everyone — it frames the whole system. If your choices die from too few options, prioritize 2–3. If you fall in love with your own conclusions, go to 4–5. Chapter 6 is for emotionally loaded decisions; 7–8 matter most when being wrong is expensive.
The Four Villains — and the WRAP Antidote

Quaker CEO William Smithburg, fresh off his Gatorade triumph, bought Snapple for $1.8 billion largely on instinct; three years later Quaker unloaded it for $300 million. Fiascos like this share a root cause: not bad luck or bad analysis, but a biased process.

The four villains: narrow framing — defining choices too narrowly, missing options outside the spotlight; confirmation bias — collecting evidence that flatters what you already want; short-term emotion — letting temporary feelings drive permanent choices; and overconfidence — certainty about a future you cannot actually predict. Kahneman's spotlight metaphor captures the trap — "what you see is all there is" — and Benjamin Franklin's pros-and-cons "moral algebra" cannot fix it, because biased minds generate biased lists.

The evidence for process: Paul Nutt found only 29% of organizational decisions considered more than one alternative, and Dan Lovallo and Olivier Sibony's study of 1,048 business decisions showed process mattered six times more than analysis.
The antidote is WRAP: Widen your options, Reality-test your assumptions, Attain distance before deciding, Prepare to be wrong — one countermeasure per villain, unpacked in the rest of the book.
Widen Your Options: Escape 'Whether or Not'

The most common decision trap is the whether-or-not decision — treating a choice as a referendum on one option. Baruch Fischhoff's study of teenagers found roughly 65% of their decisions had no real alternatives ("Should I go to the party or not?"), and adults are barely better: Paul Nutt found whether-or-not business decisions failed 52% of the time long-term, versus 32% for decisions with multiple alternatives. Hearing "whether or not" should trigger an alarm.

Two tools force the frame open. The vanishing options test: imagine a genie deletes every option currently on the table — what do you do now? Managers stuck on "fire Anna or not" suddenly generate coaching plans, role changes, and restructures that were available all along.

Second, surface opportunity cost — what the same money, time, or attention could buy instead. In Shane Frederick's experiment, shoppers choosing whether to buy a video were reminded that not buying meant "keeping the money for other purchases"; that trivial nudge cut purchases from 75% to 55%. Comparing an option against its real alternatives, not against nothing, is the cheapest upgrade in decision making.
Multitrack — and Find Someone Who's Solved Your Problem

Multitracking means developing options simultaneously, not sequentially — AND, not OR. In Steven Dow's experiment, designers who created banner ads in parallel produced higher-performing ads than those iterating one at a time: comparison sharpens judgment, and with multiple options in play, feedback stings less because your ego isn't welded to a single idea. Kathleen Eisenhardt found Silicon Valley executives who weighed several alternatives at once decided faster, with less politicking. One or two extra real options is enough — decision paralysis (as in Iyengar and Lepper's famous 24-jam study) kicks in at far larger menus. Beware sham options ginned up to make a favorite look good, and toggle between a prevention focus (avoid loss) and promotion focus (pursue gain) — Ranjay Gulati's recession research found firms blending offense and defense outperformed.

When you can't generate options, steal them: someone has solved your problem. Look at internal bright spots (Kaiser Permanente attacked sepsis with practices already proven to work), at competitors (Sam Walton built Walmart partly by copying rivals' innovations like centralized checkout), and ladder up through increasingly distant analogies — Kevin Dunbar found top science labs ran on analogy. For recurring decisions, codify prompts into reusable playlists.
Reality-Test: Consider the Opposite

Confirmation bias is the villain that lets you feel rigorous while stacking the deck. A meta-analysis by William Hart and colleagues, covering over 8,000 participants, found people are more than twice as likely to seek information that supports their existing view. Tom Gilovich's diagnosis: when we want something to be true we ask "Can I believe this?"; when we don't, "Must I believe this?" — and we usually find what the question was designed to find.

The fix is engineered disagreement. The Vatican institutionalized a devil's advocate to argue against canonizations; GM's Alfred Sloan postponed a unanimous decision until his team could develop genuine disagreement; the Pentagon uses "murder boards" to attack proposals. If dissent won't arise naturally, appoint it.

Ask disconfirming questions — ones that could prove you wrong. In one study, buyers who asked iPod sellers pointed questions ("What problems does it have?") got honest disclosures that open-ended chat never surfaced; probing matters most when the other side has reason to shade the truth. Doctors avoid anchoring by deliberately assuming their diagnosis is wrong and asking what else fits. And Roger Martin's question — "What would have to be true for this option to be right?" — turns adversaries into joint investigators instead of debaters.
Zoom Out, Zoom In — Then Ooch

To predict how your choice will turn out, distrust the inside view — your vivid, optimistic sense of your own case — and take the outside view: the base rates of people who've made this choice before. Kahneman tells on himself: his textbook team estimated two more years of work; the base rate for such projects was seven to ten years with a 40% abandonment rate. They took eight. Ask experts too — they're poor at point predictions but excellent at base rates. Brian Zikmund-Fisher, facing a risky bone-marrow transplant, decided by hunting survival statistics rather than reassurances.
Then zoom in for a close-up: go see the reality firsthand, as in Toyota's genchi genbutsu ("go and see for yourself"). Averages plus texture beat glossy summaries and your own daydreams.

When honest prediction is impossible, stop predicting and ooch: run a small, cheap experiment before committing. Intuit's Scott Cook champions "leadership by experiment" — a modest trial of a text-message crop-price service for Indian farmers (Fasal) validated a business no debate could have settled. Shadow a nurse before nursing school; give a candidate a work sample, since interviews predict performance poorly. Caveat: ooching is procrastination when the decision truly demands commitment or the evidence is already in.
Attain Distance: 10/10/10 and Core Priorities

Short-term emotion doesn't announce itself; it masquerades as conviction. In 1985, with Intel bleeding cash in memory chips, Andy Grove asked Gordon Moore: "If the board brought in a new CEO, what would he do?" Get out of memories, obviously — so they walked out the door, walked back in, and did it. Distance dissolved the emotion and history that had paralyzed them.

Suzy Welch's 10/10/10 builds that distance on demand: how will I feel about this in 10 minutes, 10 months, 10 years? The subtler enemies are the mere exposure effect (Robert Zajonc: we prefer whatever is familiar) and loss aversion (losses hurt roughly twice as much as equivalent gains feel good) — together they manufacture a powerful status quo bias that makes the familiar feel safe rather than merely familiar. The single best question: "What would I tell my best friend to do?" Advice to others is cleaner because it strips out our situational noise.

When a decision still agonizes, the conflict is usually between core priorities. Interplast's board fight over visiting surgeons versus local doctors dissolved once they named the real customer: the patient. Enshrine your priorities, write a Jim Collins-style stop-doing list, and defend calendar time — urgent trivia will otherwise crowd out what you claim matters most.
Prepare to Be Wrong: Bookend the Future

We are wildly overconfident forecasters: in studies comparing diagnoses to autopsies, physicians who were "completely certain" were wrong about 40% of the time. Since you can't predict the future, prepare for a range of futures — bookend it, planning for both the ugly low end and the surprising high end instead of a single point estimate.

For the low bookend, run Gary Klein's premortem: "It's a year from now; our decision failed spectacularly — why?" This prospective hindsight works: Mitchell, Russo, and Pennington found it boosts the number of reasons people generate by roughly 30%. For the high bookend, run a preparade: what if this succeeds beyond all expectation — are we ready? Minnetonka's Robert Taylor, launching Softsoap, bought up the national supply of plastic pumps, locking out copycats for over a year.
Build in safety factors the way engineers do — elevator cables are built about eleven times stronger than needed; software teams pad schedules with buffers. Pad your own estimates the same way.

Finally, vaccinate against predictable pain: John Wanous's work on realistic job previews shows that honestly warning recruits about a job's downsides reduces turnover — a small dose of negative truth inoculates against later shock.
Set Tripwires — and Trust the Process

Many bad outcomes come not from bad decisions but from no decision — autopilot. Kodak drifted past decades of digital-photography signals because no moment ever forced a reckoning. A tripwire is a pre-set trigger that snaps you awake: a date, a metric, a budget line. Van Halen's David Lee Roth buried a no-brown-M&Ms clause in contracts — one brown M&M backstage meant the venue hadn't read the safety-critical rider, triggering a full inspection. Zappos pays new hires to quit after training, converting drift into a conscious choice to stay.

Deadlines and partitions work the same way: in Amar Cheema and Dilip Soman's study, laborers saved more when wages arrived divided into envelopes — each partition creates a decision point. Entrepreneurs can tranche funding ("if we burn this envelope without traction, we stop"). Tripwires can also be patterns: hospitals empower nurses to summon rapid-response teams on specific warning signs.

The close: trust the process. Research on procedural justice shows people accept even unfavorable outcomes when the process was fair — real voice, genuine consideration, honest explanation. Bargainers honor deals they feel were fairly struck. Decisiveness doesn't come from certainty; it comes from knowing you widened, reality-tested, distanced, and prepared — the full WRAP loop, each tool aimed at its villain.
End of the map. Pick the chapters that earn your time.
